(January 11, 1890 – June 25, 1963) was an American [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") executive and college men's [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") coach.

["Topps, MiLB name Players of the Year: Trautman Award winners announced for each league"](https://www.milb.com/news/gcs-7626652). Minor League Baseball (MiLB.com). November 5, 2009. [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20091108022406/http://web.minorleaguebaseball.com/news/article.jsp?ymd=20091105&content_id=7626652&vkey=news_milb&fext=.jsp) from the original on November 8, 2009. Retrieved June 9, 2010. *See also* [Baseball awards#U.S. minor leagues](/wiki/Baseball_awards#U.S._minor_leagues "Baseball awards").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American basketball coach and baseball executive (1890-1963)

For the United States Marine Corps general, see [George J. Trautman III](/wiki/George_J._Trautman_III "George J. Trautman III").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/George_Trautman "Special:EditPage/George Trautman") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["George Trautman"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22George+Trautman%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22George+Trautman%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22George+Trautman%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22George+Trautman%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22George+Trautman%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22George+Trautman%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(December 2009)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/0/06/George_Trautman.jpg/250px-George_Trautman.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Trautman.jpg)

George Trautman, 1948

**George M. "Red" Trautman** (January 11, 1890 – June 25, 1963) was an American [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") executive and college men's [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") coach.

## Ohio State

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=George_Trautman&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Ohio State")]

As an undergraduate at the [Ohio State University](/wiki/Ohio_State_University "Ohio State University"), Trautman was a three-sport letterwinner in football, basketball and baseball. After graduation, he became an assistant [athletic director](/wiki/Athletic_director "Athletic director") under [Lynn St. John](/wiki/Lynn_St._John "Lynn St. John"). As assistant athletic director, Trautman was instrumental in helping to establish the Ohio Relays.

### Basketball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=George_Trautman&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Basketball")]

When St. John gave up his basketball coaching duties, he assigned them to Trautman. Trautman held the position of men's basketball [head coach](/wiki/Head_coach "Head coach") for three years. In those three years he had an overall record of 29-33.

### Honors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=George_Trautman&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Honors")]

* Trautman was inducted into the [Ohio State Varsity O Hall of Fame](/wiki/Ohio_State_Varsity_O_Hall_of_Fame "Ohio State Varsity O Hall of Fame") in 1978.
* Trautman Field, named in honor of George Trautman, was the home field of the Ohio State baseball team from 1967 to 1996.

## Professional baseball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=George_Trautman&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Professional baseball")]

In 1933 Trautman became president of the [Columbus Red Birds](/wiki/Columbus_Red_Birds "Columbus Red Birds"), a [minor league baseball](/wiki/Minor_league_baseball "Minor league baseball") team. Three years later, Trautman was named president of the [American Association](/wiki/American_Association_(20th_century) "American Association (20th century)"), the league in which the Red Birds played. He held that position from 1936 through 1945. Trautman moved the office of the league from [Durham, North Carolina](/wiki/Durham,_North_Carolina "Durham, North Carolina") to [Columbus, Ohio](/wiki/Columbus,_Ohio "Columbus, Ohio"). In 1946 Trautman was named [General manager](/wiki/General_manager_(baseball) "General manager (baseball)") of the [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers"), succeeding [Jack Zeller](/wiki/Jack_Zeller "Jack Zeller").[[1]](#cite_note-1) After two seasons with the Tigers, Trautman was appointed as the new president of the [National Association of Professional Baseball Leagues](/wiki/National_Association_of_Professional_Baseball_Leagues "National Association of Professional Baseball Leagues"), succeeding [William Bramham](/wiki/William_G._Bramham "William G. Bramham"). Trautman held that position from [1947](/wiki/1947_in_baseball "1947 in baseball") until his death in 1963.

### Accomplishments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=George_Trautman&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Accomplishments")]

Under Trautman's leadership, a new relationship was forged between the [major leagues](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") and the minor leagues. Territorial rights, which had been in dispute, were established so that if the major leagues took over a National Association city, compensation would be due to both the existing team and its league.

### George M. Trautman Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=George_Trautman&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: George M. Trautman Awards")]

Each year, the [Topps](/wiki/Topps#Awards "Topps") Company, in conjunction with [Minor League Baseball](/wiki/Minor_League_Baseball "Minor League Baseball"), awards the *George M. Trautman Award* to the *Topps Player of the Year* in each of sixteen domestic minor leagues.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Trivia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=George_Trautman&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Trivia")]

* The Ohio State [fight song](/wiki/Fight_song "Fight song"), "[Buckeye Battle Cry](/wiki/Buckeye_Battle_Cry "Buckeye Battle Cry")," was written by Trautman's brother-in-law, [Frank Crumit](/wiki/Frank_Crumit "Frank Crumit"), a musical comedy star.
